title: ABAP CDS — Staff+ Architecture
description: CDS as the data modeling backbone of S/4HANA — not syntax reference but architectural deep dive. Associations vs JOIN, table functions, hierarchy queries, DCL row-level security, annotation-driven behavior, and performance optimization patterns.

sections.title: CDS as Design Paradigm

description.value:
CDS (Core Data Services) is not merely a view definition tool — it is a fundamental shift in how SAP architects design data models. Before CDS, ABAP data modeling was procedural: you wrote function modules that SELECTed, LOOPed, and filled internal tables. Each consumer re-joined, re-filtered, and re-calculated independently. This created a combinatorial explosion of redundant SQL, inconsistent logic, and unmaintainable code.

CDS solves this by introducing a declarative data modeling layer that lives between the HANA database and the application logic. Instead of writing procedural code to fetch data, you declare what data you need and let the framework optimize the how. This is the same conceptual leap that ORMs (like Hibernate, Entity Framework) brought to Java/.NET — but CDS goes further by being database-aware, pushing computation to HANA via SQL pushdown.

In architectural terms, CDS addresses the impedance mismatch problem — the gap between the relational data model (normalized tables, JOINs, set-based operations) and the application model (objects, associations, behaviors). CDS bridges this by allowing you to define semantic data models with associations, annotations, and metadata that the runtime can interpret. This is not a new idea — it's the same principle behind GraphQL (declarative data fetching), Prisma (declarative database access), and modern data platforms like dbt.

Staff+ Perspective: When you discuss CDS in a Staff+ interview, frame it as a platform abstraction pattern — you are building a semantic layer that decouples data consumers from data sources. This is the same thinking that drives microservice BFF (Backend for Frontend) patterns, GraphQL federation, and data mesh architectures. CDS is SAP's answer to the same problem: give consumers a declarative contract, optimize execution at the platform layer.

Calculation Engine / Plan Cache</text><text class="cds-sub" x="350" y="393">~</text></svg>

The diagram above shows how CDS sits at the center of the modern S/4HANA stack. Every consumer — Fiori application, OData service, RFC, or classic report — ultimately reads through CDS views. This centralization means that data logic is defined once and reused everywhere. Changes to the data model propagate automatically to all consumers, eliminating the inconsistencies that plagued the procedural era.

Contrast with Classic ABAP: In the pre-CDS world, every report had its own SELECT with its own JOIN logic. If you needed to change how customer data was filtered, you had to find and update every report that accessed CUSTOMER table. With CDS, you define the customer view once, apply DCL for access control, and all consumers inherit the semantics. This is the same argument that drives microservice architecture — bounded contexts, single source of truth, contract-based evolution.

decisionTree.prompt: Should this data retrieval be a CDS view, AMDP method, or Open SQL query?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this a declarative data model or a procedural transformation?" Declarative = CDS view. Procedural = AMDP. Simple read = Open SQL.

branches.condition: Declarative data model with associations and aggregations
branches.path: CDS view. Optimizer pushes predicates to HANA. ~20 lines of DDL. Use for analytical queries, OData projections, transactional read models.
AMDP. Complex set-based operations in HANA. Use for data migrations, pivot tables, window functions. Avoid when logic needs RFC calls.

branches.condition: Simple SELECT with <10K rows, subsequent ABAP processing
branches.path: Open SQL. Best when subsequent processing needs ABAP runtime (ALV, field symbols). Avoid when same data is needed by multiple consumers.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: CDS views are your semantic data model layer. AMDP is compute pushdown. Open SQL is application integration. Each has a role in the layered architecture.

sections.title: Association vs JOIN — Cardinality & Performance

description.value:
The CDS association vs JOIN decision is one of the most misunderstood topics in ABAP CDS. Both have their place, and choosing incorrectly can destroy query performance or produce incorrect results. The key insight is that associations are not syntactic sugar for JOINs — they have fundamentally different semantics.

JOIN is eager: it materializes the joined data at query execution time. The cardinality is fixed and all rows are fetched regardless of whether the consumer needs them. This is the right choice when you always need the joined data and the join cardinality is well-understood (e.g., a header + item relationship where you always need both).

Association is lazy: it defines a relationship that can be resolved on demand. The framework decides whether to resolve it via an explicit JOIN, a separate SELECT, or a path expression. This gives the optimizer room to avoid unnecessary work — if the consumer never accesses the associated entity, no work is done. Associations also enable path expressions in OData ($expand) and in ABAP SQL (using the arrow operator).

Cardinality Matters: The annotation @ObjectModel.association.type: [#TO_COMPOSITION_CHILD] or [#TO_ASSOCIATION] tells the framework about the relationship. A [1..1] association can be optimized into a single SELECT with JOIN. A [0..*] association typically requires a separate SELECT or a LEFT OUTER JOIN. Getting the cardinality wrong can cause redundant SQL roundtrips.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.aj-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:8}.aj-join{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--text-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.aj-assoc{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.aj-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:Ubuntu,system-ui;font-size:11px;font-weight:600;text-anchor:middle}.aj-sub{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:Ubuntu,system-ui;font-size:9px;text-anchor:middle}.aj-flow{stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;fill:none;marker-end:url(#arrow)}.aj-tag{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--border-default);stroke-width:1.5;rx:4}.ann-pulse{animation:pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.6}50%{opacity:1}}@keyframes flowDash{0%{stroke-dashoffset:-8}100%{stroke-dashoffset:0}}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--brand-primary)"/></marker></defs><rect class="aj-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="370"/><text class="aj-lbl" x="350" y="42">Association vs JOIN Decision Framework</text><rect class="aj-join" x="30" y="65" width="290" height="50"/><text class="aj-lbl" x="175" y="90">JOIN (Eager Resolution)</text><text class="aj-sub" x="175" y="108">Data materialized at query time</text><rect class="aj-assoc" x="370" y="65" width="290" height="50"/><text class="aj-lbl" x="515" y="90">Association (Lazy Resolution)</text><text class="aj-sub" x="515" y="108">Resolved on demand via path expr</text><text class="aj-lbl" x="350" y="150">Decision Factors</text><line x1="175" y1="155" x2="175" y2="165" class="aj-flow"/><line x1="515" y1="155" x2="515" y2="165" class="aj-flow"/><rect class="aj-tag" x="30" y="170" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="175" y="190">Always need the data? → JOIN (no lazy overhead)</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="370" y="170" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="515" y="190">Data needed conditionally? → Association</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="30" y="210" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="175" y="230">Fixed 1:1 or 1:N relationship? → JOIN</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="370" y="210" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="515" y="230">Variable N:M or optional? → Association</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="30" y="250" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="175" y="270">Need WHERE on joined table? → JOIN (filter matters)</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="370" y="250" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="515" y="270">Need $expand in OData? → Association required</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="30" y="290" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="175" y="310">High cardinality / large dataset? → JOIN</text><rect class="aj-tag" x="370" y="290" width="290" height="32"/><text class="aj-sub" x="515" y="310">Nested / deep structures? → Association</text><text class="aj-lbl" x="350" y="355">Cardinality: @ObjectModel.association.type: [#TO_COMPOSITION_CHILD, #TO_ASSOCIATION]</text></svg>

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order with Association'
define view Z_SalesOrderAssoc
  as select from I_SalesOrder as so
  association [0..*] to I_SalesOrderItem as _Items
    on so.SalesOrderID = _Items.SalesOrderID
  association [1..1] to I_BusinessPartner as _Customer
    on so.CustomerID = _Customer.BusinessPartner
{
  key so.SalesOrderID,
      so.CustomerID,
      so.TotalNetAmount,
      so.CurrencyCode,
      /* Associated data — lazy resolution */
      _Customer.CompanyName,
      _Items.ItemID
}

/* 
Staff+ Analysis:
  - Association [0..*] to _Items: Lazy — consumer decides if items needed.
  - Association [1..1] to _Customer: Framework may optimize this into JOIN.
  - Using path expr _Customer.CompanyName in SELECT forces resolution —
    optimizer will likely flatten this into an INNER JOIN.
  - For OData: $expand=_Items pulls items only when requested.
*/

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order with Explicit JOIN'
define view Z_SalesOrderJoin
  as select from I_SalesOrder as so
    inner join I_BusinessPartner as bp
      on so.CustomerID = bp.BusinessPartner
    left outer join I_SalesOrderItem as soi
      on so.SalesOrderID = soi.SalesOrderID
{
  key so.SalesOrderID,
      so.CustomerID,
      so.TotalNetAmount,
      so.CurrencyCode,
      bp.CompanyName,
      soi.ItemID
}

/*
Staff+ Analysis:
  - All data is eagerly fetched — every query loads Customer + Items.
  - If consumer only needs header, this wastes I/O.
  - Use when: bulk reporting, always-read-all-data scenarios.
  - Use when: you need WHERE on joined table columns.
*/

sections.codeBlock:
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order with Association'
define view Z_SalesOrderAssoc
  as select from I_SalesOrder as so
  association [0..*] to I_SalesOrderItem as _Items
    on so.SalesOrderID = _Items.SalesOrderID
  association [1..1] to I_BusinessPartner as _Customer
    on so.CustomerID = _Customer.BusinessPartner
{
  key so.SalesOrderID,
      so.CustomerID,
      so.TotalNetAmount,
      so.CurrencyCode,
      _Customer.CompanyName,
      _Items.ItemID
}

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Order with Explicit JOIN'
define view Z_SalesOrderJoin
  as select from I_SalesOrder as so
    inner join I_BusinessPartner as bp
      on so.CustomerID = bp.BusinessPartner
    left outer join I_SalesOrderItem as soi
      on so.SalesOrderID = soi.SalesOrderID
{
  key so.SalesOrderID, so.CustomerID,
      so.TotalNetAmount, so.CurrencyCode,
      bp.CompanyName, soi.ItemID
}

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Association vs JOIN — Performance Cost of Pattern Misuse

variables.name: Association reuse
variables.value: Consumer accesses items 30% of the time

assoc.monthlyPenalty: $0 — No cost when items not needed. When needed, 2ms latency for lazy resolution.

join.monthlyPenalty: $3,200 — All 1,000 queries fetch items. 70% of the time, data goes unused. Wastes ~350,000 row transfers/day.

calculation.breakeven: At >60% item access rate, JOIN is cheaper because lazy resolution overhead (2 additional SELECTs) exceeds eager JOIN cost. Profile actual access patterns before optimizing.

sections.title: CDS Table Functions

description.value:
CDS Table Functions bridge the gap between declarative CDS views and imperative HANA SQLScript. They allow you to write HANA-native procedural logic (SQLScript with variables, loops, exception handling) and expose the result as a CDS entity. This is the escape hatch for complex calculations that cannot be expressed declaratively — hierarchical rollups, recursive traversals, fuzzy text search, or temporal joins.

The Table Function is defined in two parts: (1) the CDS view that declares the interface, and (2) the AMDP method (or stand-alone SQLScript function) that implements the logic. The @ObjectModel annotation is critical — it tells the framework how to use this function in the context of the business object model.

When to Use Table Functions: (1) You need procedural logic that CDS views cannot express (loops, dynamic SQL, temporary tables). (2) You need to call HANA-specific features like fuzzy search, spatial functions, or predictive libraries. (3) You need a calculation engine that combines multiple data sources with complex business rules. (4) You need to integrate with legacy RFC/BAPI and expose data via CDS.

Performance Consideration: Table Functions execute on the HANA layer. The SQLScript runtime can leverage HANA's column store, parallel execution, and plan caching. However, because Table Functions are opaque to the CDS optimizer (the framework cannot see inside the SQLScript), annotations like @ObjectModel.resultSetType are critical for the framework to make correct optimization decisions.

decisionTree.prompt: When should I use a CDS Table Function instead of a regular CDS view?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Can the logic be expressed declaratively?" If the data model is a single SELECT with filters and projections, use a regular CDS view. If you need procedural SQLScript (loops, temp tables, error handling), use a Table Function.

branches.condition: Single SELECT with joins, filters, aggregations
branches.path: Regular CDS view. Declarative, optimizer-friendly, supports predicate pushdown. ~5 lines of DDL. Opaque to the optimizer inside the SQLScript.

branches.condition: Procedural logic with temp tables, loops, or dynamic SQL
branches.path: CDS Table Function. Exposes results as a CDS entity but implements via AMDP SQLScript. Use for hierarchical rollups, fuzzy search, stock projections.

branches.condition: Call HANA-specific features (PAL, APL, spatial, series)
branches.path: CDS Table Function. HANA-specific libraries are only accessible via SQLScript. The Table Function wraps these and exposes results as standard CDS.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Table Functions are the escape hatch. Use them when CDS declarative syntax is insufficient. Always add @ObjectModel.resultSetType and @ObjectModel.usageType annotations so the optimizer can plan around the opaque function call.

sections.title: CDS Hierarchy

description.value:
CDS Hierarchy is one of the most powerful but underutilized features in the ABAP CDS toolkit. It allows you to define hierarchical relationships directly in the data model — org structures, bill of materials, chart of accounts, project work breakdown structures — without writing a single line of procedural ABAP code. The hierarchy is evaluated at the database level, leveraging HANA's recursive CTE (Common Table Expression) engine.

The @Hierarchy annotation is the key. It defines the parent-child relationship, the hierarchy direction (top-down or bottom-up), and the hierarchy properties (siblings order, hierarchy level, hierarchy path). Once defined, you can query the hierarchy using the HIERARCHY() function in ABAP SQL, which returns a flattened representation with parent/child relationships, depth level, and root path.

Use Cases: (1) Org management — who reports to whom, depth of reporting chain. (2) BOM explosion — top-level assembly to individual components with quantity calculation. (3) Financial consolidation — rollup of cost centers to profit centers to company code. (4) Project planning — WBS element hierarchy with work packages and activities.

Staff+ Insight: CDS Hierarchy replaces the classic ABAP pattern of recursive function modules (e.g., RH_STRUC_GET). The key advantage is that hierarchy computation happens in the database, not in the application server. For deep hierarchies (10+ levels with thousands of nodes), this can be 10-100x faster.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: CDS Hierarchy vs Recursive ABAP — Database-Level vs App-Level Traversal

variables.name: Hierarchy depth
variables.source: Large enterprise org structure (CEO → regional VP → ... → team lead)

variables.name: Query frequency
variables.source: Org management Fiori app peak usage

cds_hierarchy.monthlyPenalty: $0 — 500 queries/h × 50ms × 730h = 10.1 hours DB time. All computation in HANA. No ABAP work process overhead.

abap_recursive.monthlyPenalty: $4,800 — 500 queries/h × 2s × 730h = 202 hours. Work process utilization spikes. Each level adds a DB round-trip.

calculation.breakeven: At 6+ levels or 10K+ nodes, CDS Hierarchy is 40x faster. Below 4 levels and 1K nodes, ABAP recursion is simpler to implement and debug.

sections.title: CDS DCL — Row-Level Security

description.value:
CDS Data Control Language (DCL) provides row-level security at the data layer. Instead of implementing authorization checks in every report, service, and function module, you define access rules once in DCL and they automatically apply to all consumers of the CDS view. This is the policy-as-code approach — security is defined declaratively, enforced centrally, and transparent to consumers.

DCL works by transparently injecting WHERE clauses (or SQL predicates) into every query against the protected CDS view. The user's authorization information (from PFCG roles, authorization objects, and values) is matched against the data in the view. If the user lacks authorization for a particular row, that row is invisible — as if it doesn't exist in the database.

ASPECT pfcg_auth: This is the most common DCL pattern. It maps PFCG authorization objects to CDS view fields. For example, if a user has authorization for company code '1000' via the F_BKPF_BUK authorization object, the DCL rule ensures they only see rows where company code = '1000'. You can combine multiple authorization objects with AND/OR logic to create complex access policies.

Staff+ Perspective: DCL implements the principle of least privilege — a fundamental security principle. It's also a textbook example of cross-cutting concern implementation: security concerns are separated from business logic and enforced at the data access layer. This is the same pattern used by tools like Apache Ranger, AWS Lake Formation, and Google Cloud IAM.

@EndUserText.label: 'Access control for Sales Orders'
@MappingRole: true
define role Z_SalesOrder_Access {
  grant select on Z_SalesOrder
    where
      /*
        ASPECT pfcg_auth: Maps PFCG authorization fields to CDS view fields.
        The authorization object F_SD_SO contains:
          - VKORG (Sales Organization)
          - VTWEG (Distribution Channel)
          - SPART (Division)
      */
      asp pfcg_auth('F_SD_SO', 'VKORG', 'VTWEG', 'SPART')
        where
          SalesOrganization = aspect.mapped(VKORG) and
          DistributionChannel = aspect.mapped(VTWEG) and
          Division = aspect.mapped(SPART);

  grant select on Z_SalesOrderItem
    where
      asp pfcg_auth('F_SD_SO', 'VKORG', 'VTWEG', 'SPART')
        where
          _SalesOrder.SalesOrganization = aspect.mapped(VKORG) and
          _SalesOrder.DistributionChannel = aspect.mapped(VTWEG) and
          _SalesOrder.Division = aspect.mapped(SPART);
}

/*
  Staff+ Notes:
  - DCL is inherited: DCL on parent view automatically applies to child views.
  - If Z_SalesOrderItem is consumed via Z_SalesOrder association,
    the DCL propagates through the association chain.
  - Use CDS test double framework with MOCK Authorization to unit test DCL.
*/

@EndUserText.label: 'Access control with multiple objects'
define role Z_PurchaseOrder_Access {
  grant select on Z_PurchaseOrder
    where
      /* AND combination — both must be satisfied */
      asp pfcg_auth('M_BEST_BSA', 'BSART', '', '')
        where DocumentType = aspect.mapped(BSART)
      and
      asp pfcg_auth('M_BEST_BUK', 'BUKRS', '', '')
        where CompanyCode = aspect.mapped(BUKRS);
}

/*
  DCL Best Practices:
  1. Always use @MappingRole: true on the view or role definition.
  2. Keep DCL roles coarse-grained — one role per authorization domain.
  3. Combine with @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK on the view.
  4. Test DCL with user simulation (transaction SU01 test mode).
  5. For performance: indexed fields in DCL predicates matter enormously.
*/

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your DCL policy is too restrictive. The sales team can't see any data because their PFCG roles don't have full authorization objects configured."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Tell the sales team to fix their PFCG roles." (no empathy, no solution)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You raise a real operational concern. DCL enforces row-level security at the database layer, which means incomplete PFCG roles result in empty result sets — not an error message. Let me address this with a tiered approach: (1) Add a DCL fallback — if no authorization values are found, grant access to a minimal default set (e.g., company code '0001') instead of blocking all rows. This prevents the 'empty screen' problem. (2) Create a DCL health check CDS view that shows users their effective authorizations — they can self-diagnose role issues. (3) Add an explicit error message via @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK that tells users 'Contact admin to assign VKORG authorization' instead of silently returning zero rows. The key design principle: DCL should enforce security boundaries, but it must also provide feedback when authorization fails."
adversarialResponse.pattern: empathy-and-tiered-fix — acknowledge the operational pain, provide multiple layers of remediation (fallback defaults, self-diagnosis, clear error messages), and reframe security as a spectrum, not a binary.

sections.title: CDS Extensibility

description.value:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud relies on CDS Extensibility to allow customers to extend standard data models without modifying SAP source code. This is a critical capability for any SaaS platform — customers need custom fields, custom logic, and custom views without creating upgrade conflicts. CDS Extensibility solves this through extension views, custom fields, and business context add-ons.

The extensibility architecture works at multiple levels: (1) Custom Fields — add new fields to standard tables using the Custom Fields app (transaction CFEND or Fiori app). The field is stored in an extension include structure and automatically appended to the relevant CDS views. (2) Extension Views — create a custom CDS view that extends a standard CDS view using the EXTEND VIEW syntax. The extension view can add new fields, calculations, or associations. (3) Business Context Add-ons — package multiple extensions into a business context that can be deployed independently.

Staff+ Insight: CDS Extensibility is SAP's answer to the open-closed principle (open for extension, closed for modification). This is a core software engineering principle that FAANG companies apply to their platform APIs, plugin systems, and extension frameworks. When you discuss extensibility in an interview, frame it as a platform design decision — how do you allow customization without sacrificing upgradeability or stability?

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your CDS extensibility architecture is over-engineered. We could just modify the SAP standard view directly and save weeks of work."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You should never modify SAP standard objects." (dogmatic, no pragmatic tradeoff)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "I understand the time pressure — modifying the standard view takes 1 day, and building an extension view takes 3 days. However, the math changes when you consider upgrades. Every SAP upgrade overwrites standard views. If you modify the standard view directly, you must reapply the change after every upgrade — that's 3 days × 4 upgrades/year × 5 years = 60 days of rework. The extension view, by contrast, survives upgrades automatically because SAP's upgrade tooling does not touch customer extension includes. Over 5 years, the extension approach costs 3 days upfront + 0 days rework = 3 days total. The modification approach costs 1 day + 60 days rework = 61 days. The breakeven is at the second upgrade. Additionally, extension views support the Clean Core strategy required for S/4HANA Cloud — without it, your system cannot pass the cloud readiness check."
adversarialResponse.pattern: quantified-tradeoff — acknowledge the short-term gain, quantify the long-term cost including upgrade cycles, show the breakeven point, and connect to strategic constraints (Clean Core, cloud readiness).

sections.title: CDS Annotation Framework

description.value:
CDS annotations are the metadata backbone of the S/4HANA stack. They transform a simple data model into an OData service, a Fiori UI, an analytical query, or a search model — without writing any additional code. The annotation framework is a declarative metadata-driven architecture: you annotate your CDS views, and the framework generates the corresponding service artifacts at activation time.

Key Annotation Domains: (1) @OData — controls OData V4 service generation: publish, entity set name, DPC/MPC class generation. (2) @UI5 — defines Fiori UI rendering: line item layout, header info, value help, facet assignment. (3) @Analytics — defines analytical queries: measures, dimensions, currency conversion, aggregation behavior. (4) @Search — enables enterprise search: searchable fields, full-text index, fuzzy search configuration. (5) @ObjectModel — defines business object semantics: composition structure, transactional behavior, authorization.

Annotation Inheritance: Annotations on referenced CDS views propagate to consuming views. This means a @Semantics.amount.currencyCode annotation on a base view will automatically be respected by all views that include that field. This is inheritance in the database layer — a powerful concept that eliminates redundant metadata.

Staff+ Perspective: The annotation framework is an example of convention over configuration, a principle popularized by Ruby on Rails and adopted by modern frameworks. The annotations provide sensible defaults (conventions) that can be overridden for specific use cases (configuration). This reduces boilerplate code while maintaining flexibility.

decisionTree.prompt: Which annotation domains should I apply to this CDS view?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Who consumes this view?" Fiori UI needs @UI.* annotations. OData services need @OData.publish. Analytics needs @Analytics.*. Enterprise search needs @Search.*.

branches.path: Add @UI.lineItem, @UI.headerInfo, @UI.facet. These drive Fiori elements table and form layouts. One annotation change rebuilds the DPC/MPC at activation.
Add @Analytics.query: true with @Analytics.measure and @Analytics.dimension annotations. Enables aggregation and currency conversion at the database level.
Add @Search.searchable: true with @Search.fuzziness and @Search.decomposition. Eliminates custom search index builds.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Annotations implement convention-over-configuration. @OData.publish: true eliminates OData service creation entirely. One annotation change => full DPC/MPC regeneration at activation.

sections.title: CDS Performance Patterns

description.value:
CDS performance optimization at Staff+ level requires understanding how the HANA optimizer processes CDS views. The optimizer does not treat CDS views as black boxes — it understands their structure and can optimize across view boundaries. However, certain patterns force the optimizer to abandon optimization and fall back to procedural execution. The key is understanding which patterns are optimizer-friendly and which are not.

Union vs Subquery: UNION ALL in CDS creates a combined result set that the optimizer can handle efficiently — it pushes predicates down to each branch independently. Subqueries (especially correlated subqueries) force the optimizer to execute the inner query for each row of the outer query. If you must use a subquery, ensure the inner query is highly selective. In general, prefer UNION ALL over OR in WHERE clauses — the optimizer can parallelize UNION branches but not OR conditions.

View Decomposition: If a CDS view is too complex (many tables, nested associations, multiple layers of views), the optimizer may give up on global optimization and execute each view independently. This is the optimizer surrender point. Break complex views into layers of abstraction, but keep each layer focused. A view with more than 15–20 table references should probably be split.

Parameterization: CDS views with mandatory parameters (WHERE conditions based on input parameters) are highly optimizer-friendly because the parameter values are known at query compile time. Views without parameters force generic execution plans. Use @Environment.systemField for common parameters like system date or client.

Incorrect Cardinality: This is the most common performance bug. If you declare association [1..1] but the actual data has 0 records for the associated entity, the optimizer may generate an INNER JOIN that filters out valid rows. Always ensure annotation cardinality matches actual data cardinality.

1.5s linear infinite}@keyframes flowDash{0%{stroke-dashoffset:-8}100%{stroke-dashoffset:0}}.ann-pulse{animation:pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes fill="var(--brand-primary)"/></marker></defs><rect class="perf-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="370"/><text class="perf-lbl" x="350" y="42">CDS Performance Optimization Patterns</text><rect x="350" y="232">Full table scan, no index use</text><rect class="perf-bad" x="470" y="195" width="200" height="45"/><text class="perf-lbl" x="570" y="215">Nested Table Functions</text><text class="perf-sub" x="570" y="232">Opaque to optimizer</text><text class="perf-lbl" x="350" y="280">Key Metrics</text><text class="perf-sub" x="350" y="298">SQL Plan Cache: Check with /CDS/SQL_CACHE_MONITOR</text><text class="perf-sub" x="350" y="314">View Complexity Score: Number of tables + views + associations</text><text class="perf-sub" x="350" y="330">Predicate Pushdown: % of WHERE clauses pushed to base tables</text><text class="perf-sub" x="350" y="346">Association Resolution: Eager vs lazy — monitor SAP ASE plan</text></svg>

@EndUserText.label: 'Performance-Optimized CDS View'
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK

@Environment.systemField: #SYSTEM_DATE
define view Z_OptimizedSales
  with parameters
    P_SalesOrg   : vkorg,
    P_MaxAmount  : netwr
  as select from I_SalesOrder as so
    inner join I_Customer as c
      on so.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
{
  key so.SalesOrderID,
      c.CustomerName,
      so.TotalNetAmount,
      so.CurrencyCode,
      /*
        Parameters enable predicate pushdown:
        - WHERE P_SalesOrg filters at the table scan level
        - P_MaxAmount avoids loading large amounts into memory
      */
      cast( so.TotalNetAmount as abap.fltp ) as AmountFloat
}
where
  so.SalesOrganization = $parameters.P_SalesOrg and
  so.TotalNetAmount <= $parameters.P_MaxAmount

/*
  Performance Anti-Pattern: Using OR with non-indexed fields
  BAD:
    where so.Status = 'A' or so.Status = 'B'
  BETTER (UNION ALL):
    define view Z_StatusA as ... where so.Status = 'A'
    define view Z_StatusB as ... where so.Status = 'B'
    define view Z_Combined as select from Z_StatusA
    union all select from Z_StatusB
  
  BEST: Parameterize the status:
    with parameters P_Status: status_id
    where so.Status = $parameters.P_Status
*/

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your CDS view is slow. A simple SELECT on the table takes 200ms, but your CDS view takes 12 seconds."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "The CDS view is just a definition — it shouldn't be slow. Maybe HANA is having issues." (defensive, blames infrastructure)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Let me systematically diagnose the performance regression. A 60x slowdown suggests the CDS optimizer has surrendered — it's no longer pushing predicates down to the base tables. I'll check three things: (1) View complexity — how many tables and nested layers? If >20 table references, the optimizer may give up on global optimization. I would decompose the view into intermediate layers. (2) Association cardinality — if a [1..1] association is actually [0..1] in data, the optimizer generates an INNER JOIN that scans unnecessary rows. I'd verify cardinality annotations match actual data distribution. (3) Parameterization — the view likely lacks mandatory parameters. Without parameters, HANA can't prune partitions or use selective indexes. I'd add @Environment.systemField for date-based pruning. Let me trace the generated SQL via ST05 PlanViz to confirm which operator is the bottleneck — it's likely a full table scan on a non-partitioned column."
adversarialResponse.pattern: systematic-diagnosis — accept the problem as valid, propose a structured root-cause analysis using specific tools (ST05, PlanViz), identify common patterns (optimizer surrender, cardinality mismatch, missing parameters), and offer concrete fixes.

details: Staff+ Insight: CDS is how FAANG-scale SAP architects think about data — declarative, annotation-driven, database-pushdown. Interviewers probing your understanding of CDS want to see that you grasp declarative data modeling, not just view creation syntax. Frame CDS discussions in terms of the impedance mismatch problem and how CDS solves it.
